english: Fried Flat Rice Noodles with Meat

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kway Teow Goreng; closest parent 'fried noodle food product' (FOODON_03311069) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodles, stir-fried) is the closest defensible parent category; no specific Kway Teow Goreng entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Kway Teow Goreng with Meat; SNOMED CT does not cover this specific dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Kway Teow Goreng with Meat weighs approximately 350 g, as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker stall or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Char Kway Teow (Fried Flat Rice Noodles with Meat) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Char Kway Teow with mixed seafood and meat variants. Values adapted from the HPB Char Kway Teow (with meat) entry and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for Mee Goreng / fried noodle dishes to confirm plausibility. Per-100g values are derived from the HPB composite dish entry for stir-fried flat rice noodles with meat.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Rice noodles and stir-fried noodle analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kway Teow Goreng with Meat. GI of 50 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (stir-fried), which reports GI values in the range 40–55 depending on preparation. A midpoint of 50 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 68.25) / 100 = 34.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 19.5 × 3.5 = 68.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Kway Teow Goreng with Meat due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the recommended 2000 mg limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~50) and high glycemic load per serving (~34) due to refined flat rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Kway Teow Goreng with Meat in controlled portions, as the refined rice noodle base produces a moderate glycemic index but a high glycemic load per standard serving, which may cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, moderate saturated fat, and cholesterol from meat and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Kway Teow Goreng with Meat due to its elevated sodium content (~1680 mg per serving), moderate saturated fat (~6.3 g per serving), and cholesterol (~193 mg per serving), all of which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kway Teow Goreng with Meat is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean stir-fried noodle dish made from flat rice noodles (kway teow) wok-fried over high heat with meat (typically chicken or beef), egg, bean sprouts, chives, garlic, and a combination of dark and light soy sauce. The dish is characterised by its smoky wok-hei aroma, savoury-sweet flavour profile, and soft yet slightly chewy noodle texture. It is a staple hawker dish found across Malaysia and Singapore, often served as a main meal at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 570 kcal, 25 g protein, 24 g fat, and 68 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1680 mg per serving) due to soy sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The refined rice noodle base gives a moderate glycemic index (~50) but a high glycemic load per serving (~34), which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is generally halal when prepared with permissible meat and is dairy-free. It contains egg and soy allergens. The Malaysian variant (Kway Teow Goreng) may differ from the Singaporean Char Kway Teow in the use of dark soy sauce proportions and the inclusion of cockles or prawns in the Singaporean version; the meat-only variant described here is common in Malaysian halal hawker settings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kway Teow Goreng with Meat?
Is Char Kway Teow with meat halal?
What is the GI of fried kway teow?

Is Kway Teow Goreng gluten free?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for Char Kway Teow with meat and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD fried noodle entries. No exact HPB entry for 'Kway Teow Goreng with Meat' (Malaysian variant) was found; values are adapted from the closest HPB Char Kway Teow composite entry. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD source databases for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the primary or cross-reference sources used. The Malaysian Kway Teow Goreng variant served at New Shah Alam (Circular Road, Singapore) is a halal meat-only preparation distinct from the traditional Singaporean Char Kway Teow which typically includes cockles and pork lard. Nutrient values reflect the meat-only stir-fried flat rice noodle preparation. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific dish is not available in approved sources.
